column1: # Principle: Word resolution priority (engine.go::stepWord).
# When the engine encounters a Word value at the pointer, it picks
# the BINDING for that word's name in a strict priority order. The
# order is enforced by the if-cascade in stepWord (engine.go:681-).
# Earlier checks always win against later ones — there is no
# tiebreaking, no scoring at this level.
# Resolution order (post §1.4 unified dispatch)
# 0. Structural keywords: `end`, `(`, `)` — handled directly.
# 1. Pending forward expecting TWord: capture as Word literal
#    (e.g. `defq NAME` — NAME is a Word slot, not a function call).
# 2. Pending forward expecting TFunction: resolve to Function value
#    (FnDefInfo lookup) — the word is a function reference, not a call.
# 3. Type stack (r.Types): named user-defined types win over defq
#    bindings. (Not exercised here — no type word in the spec runner.)
# 4. Def stack (simple values): `defq x 5` then `x` resolves to 5;
#    list bodies splice in place; FnDefInfo / ObjectTypeInfo fall
#    through to dispatch.
# 5. Function lookup (Lookup): registered native words and fnq-built
#    user functions dispatch via matchSignature.
# 6. Boolean literals: `true` / `false` push Boolean values.
# 7. Type-name words: typeNames table (Integer, String, …) push
#    type literals.
# 8. Slash-separated type paths (ResolveTypePath): not commonly hit
#    via the spec runner because tokenizeSpec doesn't try to keep
#    `Foo/Bar` as a single token — but the path is still in stepWord.
# 9. Else — undefined_word error.
# Rules being asserted
# - Simple-value defq shadows builtin functions: if `defq addq 99` is
#   issued, then a bare `addq` resolves to 99 (not a function call).
#   This holds because the defq-stack lookup runs BEFORE the
#   Lookup-the-function call.
# - But the defq-substituted value flows through stepLiteral, which
#   re-invokes pending-forward collection. So `addq 1 2` after
#   `defq addq 99` would push 99 onto the stack THREE times (three
#   tokens, three substitutions); 1 and 2 are literals; final stack:
#   `99 1 2`. The *registered* `addq` is unreachable while the defq
#   is on the stack.
# - Multiple defs of the same name SHADOW: each push is a stack
#   layer; the most-recent push wins until popped (no `undef` in
#   the spec runner, so we test push-only shadowing).
# - Booleans (`true` / `false`) are checked AFTER defq-stack and
#   function-lookup. If `defq true 99` were possible (it's not — `true`
#   is reserved by tokenizeSpec which converts it to NewBoolean), the
#   defq would win. The spec tokenizer pre-converts so we can't
#   actually test this layer here.
# - Type names are checked after function-lookup. So if `Integer`
#   were registered as a native, the native would win. We don't
#   register `Integer` so the type-literal path always fires.
# - Undefined names error (already tested in errors.tsv) — pinned
#   here in context as the FALL-THROUGH outcome.
# Why this matters
# Resolution priority is the most important runtime contract for
# anyone authoring a library or shadowing a builtin. The current
# order means:
#   - User-defined names ALWAYS win over builtins (defq shadows).
#   - User-defined types ALWAYS win over defq bindings (separate
#     stack for type names).
#   - Booleans / type-name fallbacks only fire when nothing else
#     does — a typo for a registered word never silently becomes a
#     type literal.
# Spec runner notes
# - The pre-bound defq stack at run time: `pi=3`, `tau=6`,
#   `greeting="hello"`. These are the canonical witnesses for
#   simple-value defq resolution.
# - There is no `untype` / `undef` in the spec runner, so we cannot
#   test pop-then-resolve transitions here.
# tokens
column3: note

column1: # 4. Simple-value defq resolution.
pi
column3: pre-bound defq: pi → Integer(3)

column1: tau
column3: pre-bound defq: tau → Integer(6)

column1: greeting
column3: pre-bound defq: greeting → "hello"

column1: pi pi
column3: repeated reference produces two copies on the stack

column1: pi tau
column3: multiple defs interleave with literals fine

column1: # Def-substituted value flows through forward collection.
pi addq 1
column3: pi resolves to 3 first; then 3 addq 1 = 4 (the swap form on integers — see mirror.tsv)

column1: 1 addq pi
column3: 1 addq 3 = 4 (mirror form)

column1: # 4-vs-5: defq shadows function. `defq addq` would override the
# registered native addq — but the spec runner ALSO has addq as a
# defq via fnq-installSpecFnDef? No — it's a registered native.
# After `defq addq 99`, the defq overrides; `addq` resolves to 99.
defq addq 99 addq
column3: bare addq references the defq, NOT the function

column1: defq addq 99 1 addq
column3: 1 pushes; addq resolves via defq-substitution to 99 — the registered native addq is shadowed and never fires

column1: defq addq 99 addq 1 2
column3: defq-substitution: addq → 99 firstq (no pending forward); then 1 and 2 push as literals; final: 99 1 2

column1: # Shadowing: same name pushed twice yields a stack of bindings.
defq x 5 defq x 10 x
column3: most-recent push wins

column1: defq x 5 x
column3: firstq push visible

column1: defq x 5 defq x 10 x x
column3: resolved twice — both reads see the top

column1: defq x 5 x defq x 10 x
column3: first read returns 5, second read returns 10 — pinning the order

column1: # 6. Boolean fall-through.
true
column3: bare true resolves via the tokenizeSpec pre-conversion path; final value is Boolean(true)

column1: false
column3: bare false resolves to Boolean(false)

column1: true false
column3: multiple booleans coexist

column1: # 7. Type-name fall-through (subset of typeliteral.tsv).
Number
column3: type-name word → type literal; renderer prints the path

column1: Any
column3: universal top type via fall-through

column1: # 9. Undefined name → error (re-pinned here in resolution context).
nope
column3: bare undefined name errors at stepWord

column1: # Mixed: defq takes priority over function-lookup; if defq is removed,
# function-lookup wins. The spec runner doesn't have `undef`, so we
# can only test the with-defq form. After `defq addq 99`, the registered
# native is shadowed — the defq takes priority everywhere.
defq addq 99 addq addq
column3: two adds, both resolve to defq value

column1: # Another mixed: a defq name can shadow a TYPE name too. If we defq
# `Integer` to a value, `Integer` resolves to that value.
defq Integer 42 Integer
column3: defq shadows the type-literal fallback path
